title: Topic 418: The Psychology of Alternate History Games: What-If Scenarios
content:
**Topic 418: The Psychology of Alternate History Games: What-If Scenarios**

**ABSTRACT**

This paper delves into the fascinating and often bizarre world of alternate history gaming, exploring the psychological underpinnings of our obsession with “what-if” scenarios. We examine why players derive such profound satisfaction from rewriting history, whether it involves leading the Roman Empire to world domination or fighting off Nazi dinosaurs. Through a rigorous, albeit occasionally tongue-in-cheek, analysis of counterfactual thinking, cognitive dissonance, and the paradox of choice, we argue that alternate history games are more than mere escapism. They are, in fact, a vital tool for understanding our own anxieties about the past, present, and the terrifying prospect of a future where jetpacks are not yet a common mode of transportation. This paper will demonstrate that by embracing the absurd, we can gain surprisingly serious insights into the human condition, consumer behavior, and why, against all odds, we continue to believe that this time, we can totally win the war for the Allies by 1942.

**INTRODUCTION**

Since the dawn of storytelling, humanity has been captivated by the tantalizing question: “What if?” What if the asteroid missed? What if Napoleon had a better tailor? What if, instead of inventing the wheel, early humans had focused their efforts on developing a really comfortable armchair? This innate curiosity about alternate possibilities, known in academic circles as counterfactual thinking, has found its most potent and interactive expression in the realm of video games. Alternate history games, a burgeoning subgenre of interactive entertainment, allow players not just to ponder these questions, but to live out their consequences in glorious, high-definition detail.

From the grand strategy epics that let you guide a fledgling nation to global supremacy, to the first-person shooters that pit you against technologically advanced Nazi regimes, these games offer a unique form of psychological catharsis. They provide a sandbox where we can explore the roads not taken, right the wrongs of the past, and, most importantly, see what happens when you give a medieval army access to laser guns. This paper will argue that the appeal of these games goes far beyond simple power fantasies. They are a reflection of our deep-seated need to make sense of a chaotic world, to impose order on the seemingly random events of history, and to grapple with the unsettling knowledge that our own lives are a product of countless, often arbitrary, turning points.

By examining the psychological mechanisms at play, from the cognitive satisfaction of seeing a well-laid plan come to fruition to the emotional resonance of preventing a historical tragedy, we can begin to understand why millions of people spend countless hours meticulously managing the economy of an alternate-reality British Empire or leading a rebellion against a dystopian government that has outlawed cheese. This paper will explore the humorous, the profound, and the occasionally disturbing implications of our love affair with alternate histories, and in doing so, shed light on the very nature of choice, consequence, and the enduring power of a good “what-if” story.

**LITERATURE REVIEW**

The academic world, in its infinite wisdom, has not been entirely silent on the matter of alternate realities and what-if scenarios. While the specific sub-sub-field of “Psycho-Ludic Alternate Historiography” has yet to be formally established (we’re working on it), a considerable body of research in psychology and media studies provides a solid foundation for our inquiry. The most relevant of these is the study of **counterfactual thinking**, a concept that sounds like it was invented by a committee of science fiction authors but is, in fact, a legitimate area of psychological research. Roese and Morrison (2009) define counterfactuals as “mental constructions of alternatives to past events,” which is a fancy way of saying “daydreaming about what you should have said in that argument three weeks ago.” [1]

Psychologists have identified two main flavors of counterfactual thinking: upward and downward. **Upward counterfactuals** are the “if onlys” that haunt our waking moments – “if only I had invested in that obscure cryptocurrency,” “if only I hadn’t eaten that entire pizza.” These thoughts, while often leading to feelings of regret, can also be a powerful motivator for self-improvement. They are the mental equivalent of a stern but well-meaning coach, constantly reminding us that we could have done better. **Downward counterfactuals**, on the other hand, are the comforting thoughts of “it could have been worse.” They are the psychological equivalent of a warm blanket and a cup of tea, reassuring us that, while things may not be perfect, at least we didn’t accidentally join a circus. This emotional regulation function of counterfactual thinking is crucial to understanding the appeal of alternate history games. When we play a game where the Nazis have won World War II, we are engaging in a form of large-scale downward counterfactual thinking, which, paradoxically, can make us feel better about our own, less dystopian reality.

Of course, the academic world is not without its sense of humor, albeit a very dry and heavily footnoted sense of humor. In his seminal work, *The Man in the High Castle*, Philip K. Dick explored the psychological impact of living in an alternate reality where the Axis powers were victorious. [2] While not a formal academic paper, Dick’s novel is a powerful thought experiment that has influenced countless scholars and game designers. It raises profound questions about the nature of identity, the construction of history, and the human capacity for hope in the face of overwhelming despair. More recently, scholars like Lizardi (2014) have begun to examine the ways in which video games like the *BioShock* series encourage “complex historical interpretations as opposed to simplistic accepted histories.” [3] These games, with their intricate narratives and morally ambiguous choices, challenge players to think critically about the past and to question the official version of events. They are, in a sense, interactive thought experiments that allow us to explore the messy, complicated, and often contradictory nature of history.

**ANALYSIS**

So, why do we spend our precious free time meticulously managing the tax rates of a fictional 16th-century empire or leading a desperate charge against a horde of cybernetically enhanced velociraptors? The answer, we propose, lies in a potent cocktail of psychological drivers, each more fascinating and slightly more concerning than the last.

At the heart of the matter is the concept of **cognitive dissonance**, a term that sounds like a failed 80s synth-pop band but is actually a cornerstone of modern psychology. Coined by Leon Festinger in 1957, cognitive dissonance refers to the mental discomfort we experience when holding two or more contradictory beliefs, ideas, or values. [4] For example, the belief that “I am a smart, capable person” and the knowledge that “I just spent three hours trying to figure out how to build a virtual shed” can create a certain amount of psychological tension. Alternate history games provide a perfect outlet for resolving this tension. By allowing us to succeed where historical figures have failed, to make the “right” choices and achieve a more favorable outcome, these games reinforce our sense of agency and competence. They are a safe space where we can be the brilliant military strategist, the wise and benevolent ruler, or the plucky resistance fighter who single-handedly topples a global empire. In the world of the game, we are not just a person who forgot to take the bins out; we are the savior of the free world.

Another key psychological driver is the **paradox of choice**, a concept popularized by psychologist Barry Schwartz. [5] In our modern world, we are bombarded with an overwhelming number of choices, from the trivial (which of the 47 varieties of artisanal mustard should I buy?) to the life-altering (which career path will lead to a lifetime of fulfillment and a reasonably priced mortgage?). This abundance of choice, while seemingly a good thing, can often lead to anxiety, indecision, and a nagging sense of regret. Alternate history games, in a strange and beautiful way, offer a solution to this problem. They present us with a series of clear, high-stakes choices with immediate and tangible consequences. There is no ambiguity, no hemming and hawing. You either fund the development of giant steam-powered robots, or you don’t. You either form an alliance with the mole-people, or you face the consequences. This simplification of choice, this reduction of life’s complexities to a series of binary decisions, can be incredibly liberating. It is a form of escapism, yes, but it is also a form of mental decluttering, a way of temporarily silencing the endless “what ifs” that plague our daily lives.

Finally, we cannot ignore the simple, primal satisfaction of **seeing a plan come together**. The human brain is a pattern-seeking machine. We are hardwired to find order in chaos, to connect the dots and create a coherent narrative out of the random noise of existence. Alternate history games are the ultimate narrative-building tool. They allow us to craft our own stories, to shape the course of history according to our own grand designs. There is a deep, almost visceral pleasure in watching a carefully laid plan unfold, in seeing the seeds of a decision planted in the early game blossom into a glorious, world-spanning empire. It is the same satisfaction a watchmaker feels when the final gear clicks into place, the same pride an artist feels when the last brushstroke completes the masterpiece. It is the feeling of being in control, of being the master of one’s own destiny, even if that destiny is entirely fictional and involves an alarming number of laser-toting bears.

**REAL-WORLD EXAMPLES**

To truly appreciate the psychological depth and cultural significance of alternate history gaming, we must venture into the wild and wonderful world of specific examples. These are not just games; they are interactive case studies in the human psyche, each one a unique and often hilarious exploration of our collective hopes, fears, and a deep-seated desire to see what would happen if we gave Abraham Lincoln a jetpack.

Consider the **_Wolfenstein_** series, a franchise that has been gleefully mowing down Nazis since the early 1980s. The premise is simple and terrifying: the Nazis have won World War II, and the world is a very, very unpleasant place. The player takes on the role of B.J. Blazkowicz, a square-jawed American hero with a penchant for one-liners and a seemingly endless supply of ammunition. The psychological appeal of _Wolfenstein_ is twofold. First, it is a classic example of downward counterfactual thinking. By immersing ourselves in a world that is demonstrably worse than our own, we can’t help but feel a little better about our own reality. Sure, we may have to deal with traffic jams and tedious paperwork, but at least we’re not fighting giant robot dogs in the ruins of New York City. Second, the game provides a powerful sense of catharsis. It allows us to vicariously punch history’s greatest villains in their smug, jackbooted faces. In a world where real-life evil can often seem abstract and insurmountable, the simple, unambiguous act of shooting a Nazi in a video game can be surprisingly therapeutic.

Then there is the **_BioShock_** series, a franchise that is less about punching Nazis and more about pondering the philosophical implications of objectivism and the genetic modification of sea slugs. The first two games are set in the underwater city of Rapture, a libertarian utopia gone horribly wrong. The third game, _BioShock Infinite_, takes place in the floating city of Columbia, a jingoistic, hyper-religious society with a dark secret. The _BioShock_ games are a masterclass in environmental storytelling, using the game world itself to tell a complex and often disturbing story about the dangers of unchecked ideology. They are also a fascinating exploration of the concept of American exceptionalism, a theme that is both celebrated and brutally deconstructed throughout the series. The psychological appeal of _BioShock_ lies in its ability to make us think. It is a game that asks difficult questions and refuses to provide easy answers. It is a game that stays with you long after you’ve put down the controller, a testament to the power of interactive storytelling to engage with complex historical and philosophical ideas.

Finally, we have the grand strategy games, a genre that is to history what a flight simulator is to aviation. Games like **_Crusader Kings_**, **_Europa Universalis_**, and **_Hearts of Iron_** allow players to take control of a nation and guide it through centuries of war, diplomacy, and the occasional royal scandal. These games are a paradise for micromanagers and armchair generals, a place where you can spend hours agonizing over the tax policies of your 15th-century Portuguese empire or the supply lines of your panzer divisions in the Ardennes. The psychological appeal of these games is rooted in our desire for control and our fascination with complex systems. They are the ultimate sandbox, a place where we can experiment with history, test out different strategies, and see if we have what it takes to build a lasting empire. They are also a powerful reminder of the contingent nature of history, of the countless small decisions and random events that can have world-altering consequences. In the world of grand strategy, a single, well-placed marriage can be more powerful than a thousand swords, a lesson that many a real-life monarch has had to learn the hard way.

**DISCUSSION**

The implications of our analysis extend far beyond the digital realm. The same psychological drivers that make alternate history games so compelling can also be seen at play in our consumer habits and our engagement with the real world. The desire to resolve cognitive dissonance, for example, is a powerful force in advertising. When a company tells us that buying their product will make us smarter, more attractive, or more successful, they are offering us a way to bridge the gap between our current selves and our ideal selves. The product becomes a tool for self-improvement, a way of rewriting our own personal history.

The paradox of choice is another area where the lessons of alternate history gaming can be applied. In a world of endless options, we are drawn to brands and products that offer a sense of clarity and purpose. We want to be told what to buy, what to watch, and what to believe. This is why curated subscription boxes, personalized recommendations, and lifestyle influencers have become so popular. They offer a way of outsourcing our decision-making, of simplifying the complexities of modern life into a series of easy, pre-approved choices. In a sense, we are all looking for our own personal grand strategist, someone to guide us through the treacherous landscape of consumer culture.

Finally, the satisfaction of seeing a plan come together is a key driver of brand loyalty. When we invest our time, money, and emotional energy into a particular product or service, we want to see that investment pay off. We want to feel like we are part of a winning team, that our choices have been validated. This is why companies spend so much time and money building a sense of community around their brands, why they create loyalty programs and exclusive content for their most dedicated customers. They are tapping into our deep-seated need for narrative closure, for the feeling that we are part of a story with a happy ending.

**CONCLUSION**

In conclusion, alternate history games are more than just a frivolous pastime for history buffs and aspiring dictators. They are a funhouse mirror that reflects our own anxieties, desires, and a deep-seated need to make sense of a chaotic world. They are a playground for the mind, a place where we can explore the roads not taken and rewrite the stories of our past. By embracing the absurd, by indulging our fantasies of a world where the Roman Empire never fell or where the Aztecs conquered Europe, we can gain surprisingly serious insights into the human condition.

These games remind us that history is not a fixed, monolithic entity, but a fluid, ever-changing narrative. They teach us that our choices have consequences, that a single moment can change the course of a lifetime. And, perhaps most importantly, they remind us that we are all the authors of our own stories, that we all have the power to shape our own destinies. So the next time you find yourself leading a cavalry charge against a horde of alien invaders or negotiating a trade agreement with a race of sentient fungi, remember that you are not just playing a game. You are engaging in a profound act of psychological exploration, a journey into the heart of what it means to be human. And you are also, in all likelihood, having a ridiculous amount of fun.
references:
[1] Roese, N. J., & Morrison, M. (2009). The psychology of counterfactual thinking. Historical Social Research/Historische Sozialforschung, 34(2), 16-26.
[2] Dick, P. K. (1962). The man in the high castle. G. P. Putnam's Sons.
[3] Lizardi, R. (2014). BioShock: Complex and alternate histories. Game Studies, 14(1).
[4] Festinger, L. (1957). A theory of cognitive dissonance. Stanford university press.
[5] Schwartz, B. (2004). The paradox of choice: Why more is less. Ecco.
